explanation_en: 'Inhalt' means 'content'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.de: _____ Inhalt des Films ist sehr spannend.
question.en: The content of the film is very exciting.

explanation_en: 'Inhalt' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ des Buches ist sehr interessant.
question.en: The content of the book is very interesting.

explanation_en: 'Inhalt' means 'content'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.en: We are discussing the content of the article.

explanation_en: 'besprechen' takes the accusative. 'den Inhalt' — masculine accusative uses 'den'. Noun is unchanged.
'Ausdruck' means 'expression'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.de: _____ Ausdruck ist sehr typisch für die Umgangssprache.
question.en: The expression is very typical of colloquial speech.

explanation_en: 'Ausdruck' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The expression is not familiar to me.

explanation_en: 'Ausdruck' means 'expression'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Ich suche den passenden _____ auf Deutsch.
question.en: I am looking for the right expression in German.

explanation_en: 'suchen' takes the accusative. 'den passenden Ausdruck' — masculine accusative uses 'den'.
'Fehler' means 'mistake' or 'error'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.en: The mistake is easy to fix.

explanation_en: 'Fehler' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ liegt in der dritten Zeile.
question.en: The mistake is in the third line.

explanation_en: 'Fehler' means 'mistake'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Ich habe meinen _____ erkannt und korrigiert.
question.en: I recognised and corrected my mistake.

explanation_en: 'erkennen' and 'korrigieren' take the accusative. 'meinen Fehler' — masculine accusative uses 'meinen'. Noun unchanged.
'Vorteil' means 'advantage'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.de: _____ Vorteil dieser Methode ist die Schnelligkeit.
question.en: The advantage of this method is its speed.

explanation_en: 'Vorteil' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The advantage is obvious.

explanation_en: 'Vorteil' means 'advantage'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.en: That offers us a great advantage.

explanation_en: 'bieten' takes dative (uns) + accusative (Vorteil). 'einen großen Vorteil' — masculine indefinite accusative.
'Nachteil' means 'disadvantage'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.en: The disadvantage is the high price.

explanation_en: 'Nachteil' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The disadvantage of this solution is obvious.

explanation_en: 'Nachteil' means 'disadvantage'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Ich sehe einen großen _____ in diesem Plan.
question.en: I see a big disadvantage in this plan.

explanation_en: 'sehen' takes the accusative. 'einen großen Nachteil' — masculine indefinite accusative uses 'einen'.
'Einwohner' means 'inhabitant'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.de: _____ Einwohner der Stadt kennt diese Straße.
question.en: The inhabitant of the city knows this street.

explanation_en: 'Einwohner' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The inhabitant has lived here for years.

explanation_en: 'Einwohner' means 'inhabitant'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Die Stadt hat über eine Million _____.
question.en: The city has over one million inhabitants.

explanation_en: 'Einwohner' is the plural form here (same as singular). After a number, the plural is used without an article.
'Zustand' means 'condition' or 'state'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.en: The condition of the building is poor.

explanation_en: 'Zustand' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ des Autos hat sich verbessert.
question.en: The condition of the car has improved.

explanation_en: 'Zustand' means 'condition'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Wir müssen den _____ des Patienten überwachen.
question.en: We need to monitor the patient's condition.

explanation_en: 'überwachen' takes the accusative. 'den Zustand' — masculine accusative uses 'den'. Noun unchanged.
'Verlust' means 'loss'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.en: The loss was very painful.

explanation_en: 'Verlust' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ seiner Arbeit hat ihn sehr getroffen.
question.en: The loss of his job hit him very hard.

explanation_en: 'Verlust' means 'loss'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Das Unternehmen hat einen großen _____ erlitten.
question.en: The company suffered a great loss.

explanation_en: 'erleiden' takes the accusative. 'einen großen Verlust' — masculine indefinite accusative uses 'einen'.
'Fortschritt' means 'progress'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.de: _____ Fortschritt in der Wissenschaft ist beeindruckend.
question.en: The progress in science is impressive.

explanation_en: 'Fortschritt' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The progress in learning German is visible.

explanation_en: 'Fortschritt' means 'progress'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Wir feiern den _____, den wir gemacht haben.
question.en: We celebrate the progress we have made.

explanation_en: 'feiern' takes the accusative. 'den Fortschritt' — masculine accusative uses 'den'. Noun unchanged.
'Beitrag' means 'contribution'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.en: The contribution to the solution is important.

explanation_en: 'Beitrag' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The contribution of every individual counts.

explanation_en: 'Beitrag' means 'contribution'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Sie leistet einen wichtigen _____ zum Klimaschutz.
question.en: She makes an important contribution to climate protection.

explanation_en: 'leisten' takes the accusative. 'einen wichtigen Beitrag' — masculine indefinite accusative uses 'einen'.
'Zweck' means 'purpose'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.en: The purpose of this exercise is unclear.

explanation_en: 'Zweck' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ des Treffens ist die Planung.
question.en: The purpose of the meeting is planning.

explanation_en: 'Zweck' means 'purpose'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Ich sehe keinen _____ in dieser Diskussion.
question.en: I see no purpose in this discussion.

explanation_en: 'sehen' takes the accusative. 'keinen Zweck' — masculine accusative uses 'keinen'. Noun unchanged.
'Krankenhaus' means 'hospital'. It is a neuter noun taking 'das'.

question.en: The hospital is well equipped.

explanation_en: 'Krankenhaus' is neuter. As the nominative subject, it takes 'das'.

question.de: Das _____ liegt in der Nähe des Bahnhofs.
question.en: The hospital is near the train station.

explanation_en: 'Krankenhaus' means 'hospital'. It is the neuter subject, used with 'das'.

question.en: He was taken to hospital immediately.

explanation_en: 'ins' = in + das (accusative, showing movement into a place). 'ins Krankenhaus' is a fixed phrase used when going into hospital.
'Rathaus' means 'town hall'. It is a neuter noun taking 'das'.

question.en: The town hall stands on the market square.

explanation_en: 'Rathaus' is neuter. As the nominative subject, it takes 'das'.

question.en: The town hall is a historic building.

explanation_en: 'Rathaus' means 'town hall'. It is the neuter subject, used with 'das'.

question.de: Wir müssen zum _____ gehen, um die Dokumente einzureichen.
question.en: We have to go to the town hall to submit the documents.

explanation_en: 'zum' = zu + dem (dative). Neuter dative: 'dem Rathaus' → 'zum Rathaus'. The noun is unchanged.
'Flughafen' means 'airport'. It is a masculine noun taking 'der'.

question.en: The airport is very modern.

explanation_en: 'Flughafen' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ liegt 30 Kilometer von der Stadt entfernt.
question.en: The airport is 30 kilometres from the city.

explanation_en: 'Flughafen' means 'airport'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Wir müssen in zwei Stunden am _____ sein.
question.en: We have to be at the airport in two hours.

explanation_en: 'am' = an + dem (dative). Masculine dative: 'dem Flughafen' → 'am Flughafen'. Noun unchanged.
'Umwelt' means 'environment' (the natural world around us). It is a feminine noun taking 'die'.

question.en: The environment suffers from pollution.

explanation_en: 'Umwelt' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.de: Die _____ muss für die nächste Generation geschützt werden.
question.en: The environment must be protected for the next generation.

explanation_en: 'Umwelt' means 'environment'. It is the feminine subject here.

question.en: We need to better protect the environment.

explanation_en: 'schützen' takes the accusative. Feminine: 'die Umwelt' (same form in accusative).
'Energie' means 'energy'. It is a feminine noun taking 'die'.

question.en: The energy of the sun is renewable.

explanation_en: 'Energie' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.en: Energy has become more expensive.

explanation_en: 'Energie' means 'energy'. It is the feminine subject here.

question.en: We need more renewable energy.

explanation_en: After 'erneuerbare' (feminine accusative adjective), 'Energie' is unchanged — feminine nominative and accusative are identical.
'Technik' means 'technology' or 'technique'. It is a feminine noun taking 'die'.

question.en: Technology is developing very quickly.

explanation_en: 'Technik' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.en: Modern technology simplifies our lives.

explanation_en: 'Technik' means 'technology'. It is the feminine subject here.

question.en: He has mastered this technique very well.

explanation_en: 'beherrschen' takes the accusative. 'diese Technik' — feminine accusative demonstrative 'diese'. Noun unchanged.
'Gesundheit' means 'health'. It is a feminine noun taking 'die'. Also used as 'Bless you!' when someone sneezes.

question.en: Health is the most important thing.

explanation_en: 'Gesundheit' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.de: Die _____ der Bevölkerung hat sich verbessert.
question.en: The health of the population has improved.

explanation_en: 'Gesundheit' means 'health'. It is the feminine subject here.

question.en: I want to improve my health.

explanation_en: 'verbessern' takes the accusative. 'meine Gesundheit' — feminine accusative is same as nominative.
'Gewalt' means 'violence' or 'force'. It is a feminine noun taking 'die'.

question.de: _____ Gewalt ist in einer Demokratie verboten.
question.en: Violence is forbidden in a democracy.

explanation_en: 'Gewalt' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.de: Die _____ in den Nachrichten macht mich traurig.
question.en: The violence in the news makes me sad.

explanation_en: 'Gewalt' means 'violence'. It is the feminine subject here.

question.de: Wir müssen die _____ in der Gesellschaft bekämpfen.
question.en: We must fight violence in society.

explanation_en: 'bekämpfen' takes the accusative. Feminine: 'die Gewalt' (same in accusative).
'Freiheit' means 'freedom'. It is a feminine noun taking 'die'.

question.en: Freedom is a fundamental right.

explanation_en: 'Freiheit' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.en: The freedom of the press is important.

explanation_en: 'Freiheit' means 'freedom'. It is the feminine subject here.

question.de: Wir schätzen die _____, die wir haben.
question.en: We value the freedom that we have.

explanation_en: 'schätzen' takes the accusative. Feminine: 'die Freiheit' (unchanged in accusative).
'Verantwortung' means 'responsibility'. It is a feminine noun taking 'die'.

question.en: The responsibility lies with every individual.

explanation_en: 'Verantwortung' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.de: Die _____ für dieses Projekt liegt bei dir.
question.en: The responsibility for this project lies with you.

explanation_en: 'Verantwortung' means 'responsibility'. It is the feminine subject here.

question.de: Er übernimmt die _____ für sein Handeln.
question.en: He takes responsibility for his actions.

explanation_en: 'übernehmen' takes the accusative. Feminine: 'die Verantwortung' (unchanged in accusative).
